Introduction to Retrieval-Augmented Generation

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI framework that combines the strengths of retrieval-based and generative models. It enhances large language model outputs by grounding them in factual, retrieved documents.

How RAG Works

The RAG pipeline consists of three core stages. First, documents are ingested and split into overlapping text chunks. Each chunk is converted into a dense vector embedding that captures its semantic meaning.

Second, when a user submits a query, the query is also embedded into the same vector space. A similarity search finds the chunks whose embeddings are closest to the query embedding.

Third, the retrieved chunks are assembled into a context block and injected into the LLM prompt alongside the original question. The model generates an answer grounded exclusively in that context.

Benefits

RAG reduces hallucination by constraining the model to verified source material. It enables knowledge to be updated without retraining the model — simply add new documents to the vector store. It also provides source citations so answers can be audited.

Technical Stack

A typical RAG stack includes a vector database such as Supabase pgvector or Pinecone, an embedding model such as sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2, and a generative model such as Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant.
